one_line_takeaway: A largely survey/theory paper that also contributes two original formal metrics (trajectory persistence A_k, representational risk R) and a small proof-of-concept GRU/RSSM adversarial-perturbation experiment (A1=2.26x, reduced to 0.92x via PGD-10 adversarial training) partially bridged to a real but CPU-debug-scale DreamerV3 checkpoint -- useful as a taxonomy/threat-model reference for world-model security, but its empirical backing is intentionally small-scale and explicitly does not extend to production systems.
key_finding: In the GRU world model, a single adversarial perturbation at t=0 produced trajectory amplification A1 = 2.26x relative to the stateless baseline (E^WM_1 = 0.0578 +/- 0.0006 vs E^ss_1 = 0.0255 +/- 0.0005), decaying rapidly to A2=1.01x, A5=0.021x, A10=0.000126x -- an early-step-concentrated damage window that coincides with where reward-head estimation and planning occur. The stochastic RSSM proxy showed lower initial amplification, A1 = 0.65x, with slower decay (remaining in the 0.02-0.08x range through step 15), demonstrating architecture-dependence of trajectory persistence. The real DreamerV3 checkpoint probe found non-zero, front-loaded amplification (A1=0.0262, A5=4.55e-4, A10=2.39e-4, latent error E1=1.4523) with measurable action drift (||delta a1|| = 0.0080) at the same step, i.e., representational perturbations propagate into policy outputs even in a deployed RSSM stack, though the authors caveat this checkpoint result as conservative and task-limited. PGD-10 adversarial fine-tuning reduced A1 from 2.26x to 0.92x (-59.5%), with reductions of -89.3% at k=5 and -85.2% at k=10. Absolute cumulative reward at horizon H=30 differed only slightly between clean and perturbed policy (-0.5056 +/- 0.0236 vs -0.5065 +/- 0.0236; WM reward gap 0.000892 +/- 0.000057 vs SS gap 0.000175 +/- 0.000020), which the authors interpret as the GRU proxy operating in a regime of moderate rather than catastrophic reward damage, while the WM/SS gap ratio still supports trajectory persistence as a measurable downstream effect. Separately (drawn from cited literature, not the paper's own experiments), the paper reports that adversarial perturbations in multi-agent autonomous driving have achieved attack success rates up to 67% inducing chain collisions [cited as ref 54], PhysCond-WMA increases open-loop planning error by ~20% [cited as ref 21], and model extraction can replicate dynamics-model behavior with ~80% fidelity using a few thousand queries [cited as ref 61] -- these are Related Work findings, not results the authors themselves produced.
core_contribution: The paper (Parmar, SovereignAI Security Labs) presents a unified threat model for 'world models' (learned latent dynamics simulators used in robotics, autonomous driving, and agentic AI). Its own stated contributions are: (i) a survey of world model architectures and deployment contexts; (ii) a six-layer asset inventory (observation encoder, dynamics model, reward/termination heads, rollout/imagination engine, policy/actor, memory/context store); (iii) an extension of MITRE ATLAS and the OWASP LLM Top 10 to the world-model stack, including a five-profile attacker capability taxonomy (white-box, grey-box, black-box, insider, supply-chain) defined along access/knowledge/goal axes; (iv) two original formal definitions -- trajectory persistence (amplification ratio A_k = E^WM_k / E^ss_k comparing recurrent-state error propagation to a memoryless baseline) and representational risk (R(theta,D) = total-variation distance between true and learned transition distributions on a deployment distribution); (v) an original empirical proof-of-concept: adversarial perturbation experiments on a GRU-based RSSM approximation, a stochastic RSSM proxy, and checkpoint-level probing of a real (CPU debug) DreamerV3 model; (vi) four hypothetical (explicitly labeled 'not empirically validated') scenario studies in driving, robotics, enterprise automation, and social simulation; and (vii) a mitigation framework and practitioner checklist mapped to NIST AI RMF and the EU AI Act.
threat_model: Adversaries are classified along three axes -- access (full stack / API-level / input-channel-only / training pipeline / pre-training corpus), knowledge (complete/partial/none), and goal -- yielding five profiles: White-box (gradient-based encoder perturbation, dynamics poisoning; goal: targeted policy manipulation), Grey-box (black-box adversarial examples, model extraction via rollout queries; goal: model extraction/policy degradation), Black-box (transfer attacks, physical adversarial patches, supply-chain seeding via sensor/data-feed access only; goal: inducing unsafe states at inference), Insider (poisoned training batches, tampered checkpoints; goal: backdoor injection/persistent sabotage), and Supply-chain (web-scale data poisoning; goal: encoding representational bias in a foundation model). The paper maps these onto MITRE ATLAS tactics (Initial Access/Persistence via training-data poisoning, Manipulation/Impact via encoder perturbation and dynamics poisoning, Exfiltration via model inversion/extraction, Discovery/Evasion via rollout probing) and onto OWASP LLM Top 10 categories (prompt injection redirecting planning branches, excessive autonomy taking irreversible actions, supply-chain risk from foundation-model training data, sensitive information disclosure via model inversion). The paper explicitly notes that neither ATLAS nor OWASP currently addresses the dynamics/rollout layer or compounding-error risk, which is the gap it targets.
limitations: The paper's own scope note (repeated in the abstract, Section 1.1 'Scope and contribution types', and Section 7.3) states that empirical claims rest only on GRU/RSSM proxy experiments and a CPU debug DreamerV3 checkpoint on a synthetic environment ('dummy_disc'), not full-scale deployed systems; broader claims about production systems are supported by literature synthesis and theoretical analysis rather than end-to-end measurement. The four scenario studies (Section 9) are explicitly labeled hypothetical stress narratives, not empirically validated attack chains. The risk-tier thresholds for the amplification ratio A1 (e.g., A1<1.5 low concern, A1>=5 high concern) are stated by the authors to be heuristic starting points requiring architecture- and domain-specific calibration. The DreamerV3 checkpoint results are called 'conservative and task-limited'; full-scale replication on benchmark DreamerV3/SafeDreamer training runs is left as future work. Representational risk R(theta,D) is defined formally but the authors note it cannot be computed directly since the true environment distribution is unknown, so only proxies (held-out TV test, ensemble disagreement, latent OOD score) are proposed, not validated. No code or experiment artifacts are yet released (promised 'upon acceptance').

datasets_used: Synthetic Monte Carlo rollout data generated from a minimal GRU-based RSSM proxy (obs dim 32, hidden dim 64, latent dim 16), not a named public dataset
Stochastic RSSM proxy under the same synthetic protocol
DreamerV3 CPU debug checkpoint on the 'dummy_disc' environment (synthetic/dummy observations, not a benchmark task)
quantitative_results: Stochastic RSSM proxy: A1 = 0.65x, remaining in 0.02-0.08x range through step 15
Real DreamerV3 checkpoint probe: A1=0.0262, A5=4.55e-4, A10=2.39e-4, latent error E1=1.4523, action drift ||delta a1||=0.0080
PGD-10 adversarial fine-tuning mitigation: A1 reduced 2.26x -> 0.92x (-59.5%); A5 reduced by -89.3%; A10 reduced by -85.2%
Absolute cumulative reward at H=30: clean policy -0.5056+/-0.0236 vs perturbed -0.5065+/-0.0236; WM reward gap 0.000892+/-0.000057 vs SS gap 0.000175+/-0.000020
Cited (not original): autonomous-driving multi-agent adversarial perturbation attack success rate up to 67% causing chain collisions (ref 54)
Cited (not original): PhysCond-WMA increases open-loop planning error by ~20% (ref 21)
Cited (not original): model extraction achieves ~80% fidelity replication using a few thousand queries (ref 61)
baselines_compared: Stateless single-step (memoryless) re-encoding baseline vs. the recurrent GRU world model (core comparison defining A_k)
Deterministic GRU world model vs. stochastic RSSM proxy (architecture comparison)
Undefended GRU world model vs. PGD-10 adversarially fine-tuned GRU world model (mitigation comparison)
Synthetic GRU/RSSM proxy vs. real DreamerV3 checkpoint (proxy-to-deployed-architecture validation)
